cue: How do you say you're heartbroken?

es.literal: to have the heart made into pieces
es.example: Desde que se fue tiene el corazón hecho pedazos.
es.exampleTranslation: She's been heartbroken since he left.

it.literal: to have the heart in pieces
it.example: Da quando è partito ha il cuore a pezzi.
it.exampleTranslation: She's been heartbroken since he left.

bridge.note: A near-exact twin with one joint different: Spanish MAKES the heart into pieces (hecho pedazos, a past participle doing the breaking), Italian simply has it AT pieces (a pezzi, a state it is in). Spanish narrates the damage; Italian reports the condition. Everything else — organ, image, register — matches, which makes this a safe crossing and a good day to notice how small the remaining gap can be.

interference.es_to_it: 'Il cuore fatto pezzi' is wrong: Italian uses the preposition — a pezzi.
interference.it_to_es: 'Tener el corazón a pedazos' misses the participle; Spanish wants hecho pedazos.

review.notes: ES side discovered in the Spanish idioms book Joe is reading, 2026-07-21 (discovery only; all glosses and examples authored fresh here). IT counterpart from Claude's recall — UNVERIFIED. Freebie twin whose payload is the participle-vs-preposition joint. Natural Valentine's-adjacent companion to 0077.
